stimulus:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

  • Online recipe-recommendation systems suggest dishes based on user preferences.
  • Ingredient-based systems rely on the chemical compounds present in ingredients.
  • These compounds can shift dramatically depending on ripeness, sourcing, and storage.
  • Recommendation engines based on chemical profiles may therefore mismatch dishes that users actually find appealing.
  • Cuisine-tag-based systems, which classify dishes by stable cultural categories, tend to be steadier predictors of user satisfaction.
stem: The student wants to explain a drawback of ingredient-based recipe-recommendation systems. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

answerOptions.content: Several online recipe-recommendation systems rely on chemical compounds in ingredients to suggest dishes.

Because chemical compounds in ingredients can shift with ripeness and storage, recommendation systems based on them may mismatch dishes, making them less reliable than systems based on stable cuisine tags.
Ingredient-based systems describe chemical compounds, which vary with ripeness, whereas cuisine-tag systems describe stable cultural categories that predict user satisfaction.
Cuisine-tag-based systems are steadier predictors of user satisfaction because the categories they rely on do not shift over time.

rationale:
Choice B is the best answer. It identifies a specific drawback of ingredient-based systems—their reliance on shifting chemical profiles can produce mismatches—and contrasts them with the more reliable cuisine-tag systems.

Choice A is incorrect because it merely describes the systems without evaluating them. Choice C is incorrect because it describes a difference without linking that difference to a disadvantage. Choice D is incorrect because it focuses on cuisine-tag systems rather than identifying a drawback of ingredient-based ones.
